items.title:
1994-2014: Post-Shor's Algorithm Optimism
After Peter Shor demonstrates that a quantum computer could
factor large numbers, predictions converge on '10-20 years'
to practical quantum computing. The algorithm works perfectly.
The computer to run it does not yet exist.

2001-2016: IBM 7-Qubit Era
IBM successfully factors the number 15 using a 7-qubit
quantum computer. Predictions of '10-15 years' to practical
applications follow. Classical computers could factor 15
in 1952, but the press releases do not mention this.

2011-2021: D-Wave Commercial Era
D-Wave sells the first 'commercial' quantum computer.
Whether it performs quantum computation is debated.
Whether it outperforms a laptop is also debated.
Whether it qualifies as a computer is occasionally debated.

2019-2029: Google Quantum Supremacy Era
Google achieves 'quantum supremacy' on a problem specifically
designed to be hard for classical computers and easy for
quantum computers. Predictions tighten to '5-10 years.'
The problem solved has no known practical application.

2023-2033: IBM 1,000+ Qubit Era
IBM unveils a 1,121-qubit processor. Predictions remain
at '5-10 years.' The number of qubits grows exponentially.
The number of useful computations performed grows linearly,
from zero.

1994: Shor's Algorithm
Peter Shor publishes an algorithm proving that a sufficiently
large quantum computer could break RSA encryption.
The 'sufficiently large' qualifier has been doing
heroic work for three decades.

2001: IBM Factors the Number 15
A 7-qubit quantum computer correctly determines that
15 = 3 x 5. The result is verified using a pocket calculator.

2011: D-Wave One
D-Wave sells its first commercial quantum computing system
for $10 million. It occupies a 10-square-meter room
cooled to near absolute zero, conditions also found
in most open-plan offices.

2019: Google Quantum Supremacy
Google's 53-qubit Sycamore processor completes a calculation
in 200 seconds that would take a classical supercomputer
10,000 years. IBM disputes the 10,000-year claim,
estimating 2.5 days. Both numbers are impressive
for a problem nobody needed solved.

2023: IBM Condor Processor
IBM unveils the 1,121-qubit Condor processor,
the first quantum processor to exceed 1,000 qubits.
Error rates remain too high for practical computation,
but the press release is error-free.

2026: Current State
Quantum computing remains approximately 5-10 years
from economic viability, as it has been since 1994.
The technology's primary output continues to be
press releases and venture capital consumption.
